text: Hello friends, this is a case of a twenty one year old male who presented with history of right lower quadrant pain, two days and vomiting. An examination revealing severe tendon necrotite alexosa with rebound. An ultrasound examination showing a nine mm appendix.

The laparoscopic view shows the cecum with visible tinea coli, leading to the base of the appendix. The terminal ileum is lifted to reveal the appendix, which is positioned in the pelvis and adherent due to inflammation. The appendix tip is severely inflamed with contained perforation, surrounded by omentum and fibrinous flecks. The surgeon performs blunt dissection to release the appendix from pelvic adhesions, making the appendix visible.

The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to hold the appendix while dissecting the thick and edematous mesoappendix with the back of a hook. Traction and counter-traction techniques are applied to carefully separate the mesoappendix bit by bit, ensuring visibility by releasing smoke and avoiding bleeding that could obscure the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully secures a handmade 2-0 Vicryl loop around the base of the appendix, where the three tinea coli converge at the cecum. Using a Maryland dissector, the loop is pushed down to the desired position, ensuring the knot is neither too loose nor too tight to avoid avulsion. The knot is secured, creating a visible dumbbell shape, and the appendix is blanched. The surgeon then prepares to cut the appendix using either a hook or scissors, ensuring not to leave a large stump to prevent stump appendicitis.

The surgeon removes the appendix through the left iliac fossa port using a ten mm trocar, ensuring the stump is well secured. The right iliac fossa is irrigated to clean the area, and the pelvis appears clean. The trocar is removed under vision, with no port site bleeding observed.
